narrative.setting: The Magic School Bus has been miniaturized and is entering Mr. Edgar's respiratory system from the very beginning - his nose. The nasal cavity stretches before you like a vast pink tunnel, lined with tiny hairs and dripping with mucus. Ms. Frizzle gestures grandly: 'The nose! Where the respiratory journey begins. The ancient Greeks called it 'rhis' - rhinos. Every medical term with this prefix points right here.'
narrative.tension: Mr. Edgar has chronic rhinitis. Understanding nasal anatomy and terminology is key to diagnosis.

scaffolds.narrative: Ms. Frizzle pulls up ancient Greek text. 'Rhis, rhinos - the Greek word for nose. It's the root of so many medical terms. Learn this one prefix and dozens of words unlock.'
scaffolds.q: The prefix 'rhino-' comes from Greek meaning:
scaffolds.explain: 'RHINO-' = NOSE. From Greek 'rhis, rhinos' = nose. Any medical term starting with 'rhino-' involves the nose. Also gives us 'rhinoceros' (nose-horn animal)!

wrongExplains.0: Throat terms use 'pharyngo-' or 'laryngo-'. Rhino- is specifically nose.
wrongExplains.2: Lung terms use 'pneumo-' or 'pulmo-'. Rhino- points to the nose.
wrongExplains.3: Mouth terms use 'oro-' or 'stoma-'. Rhino- is the nose.

scaffolds.narrative: The bus floats through the nasal cavity. Mucus glands secrete fluid, tiny hairs (cilia) wave, turbinates swirl the air. 'All of this,' Ms. Frizzle says, 'is the domain of rhino-.'
scaffolds.q: Which structure does the prefix 'rhino-' specifically refer to?
scaffolds.explain: 'RHINO-' specifically refers to the NOSE and NASAL CAVITY - the external nose and internal nasal passages. This includes the nostrils, nasal septum, turbinates, and nasal mucosa. Not sinuses (sinus-), not lower airways.

wrongExplains.0: Sinuses use 'sinus-' in their terms (sinusitis). Rhino- is the nasal cavity itself.
wrongExplains.2: Trachea uses 'tracheo-'. Rhino- is much higher up - the nose.
wrongExplains.3: Bronchi use 'broncho-'. Rhino- refers to the nose, the first part of the airway.

scaffolds.narrative: Mr. Edgar sneezes, and the bus is nearly blown out of the nasal cavity. 'Rhinorrhea!' Ms. Frizzle shouts over the gale. 'Rhino- plus rhea. Can you decode it?'
scaffolds.explain: RHINORRHEA = RHINO (nose) + RRHEA (flow, discharge) = RUNNY NOSE. Watery nasal discharge, like in allergies or cold. '-rrhea' always means flow (diarrhea = flow through, seborrhea = oil flow).

wrongExplains.0: Nosebleed is EPISTAXIS (or rhinorrhagia). Rhinorrhea is discharge/drainage, not bleeding.
wrongExplains.2: Difficulty breathing through nose is nasal obstruction or congestion. Rhinorrhea is discharge.
wrongExplains.3: Sneezing is a reflex/symptom. Rhinorrhea specifically means fluid discharge from the nose.

scaffolds.narrative: The nasal mucosa is inflamed - red, swollen, hypersecretive. 'Rhinitis,' Ms. Frizzle diagnoses. 'Inflammation of the nasal passages. The '-itis' suffix is our clue.'
options: Inflammation of the nose / nasal mucosa
scaffolds.explain: RHINITIS = RHINO (nose) + ITIS (inflammation) = NASAL INFLAMMATION. Causes: allergies (allergic rhinitis/hay fever), infection (viral rhinitis/common cold), irritants. Symptoms: congestion, rhinorrhea, sneezing.

wrongExplains.0: Surgical removal is '-ectomy'. Rhinitis is inflammation ('-itis'), not surgery.
wrongExplains.2: Plastic surgery is RHINOPLASTY. Rhinitis is inflammation, a medical condition.
wrongExplains.3: Nosebleed is epistaxis. Rhinitis is inflammation, which may cause congestion/drainage but not bleeding.

scaffolds.narrative: A tiny light shines into the nasal cavity from outside. 'Rhinoscopy,' Ms. Frizzle explains. 'The doctor is examining Mr. Edgar's nasal passages with a scope.'
options: Visual examination of nasal cavity with a scope
scaffolds.explain: RHINOSCOPY = RHINO (nose) + SCOPY (visual examination) = LOOKING INTO THE NOSE with a scope (rhinoscope). Can be anterior (through nostrils) or posterior (through throat). '-scopy' always means visual examination.

wrongExplains.0: X-ray is radiography. Rhinoscopy uses direct visualization with light and scope.
wrongExplains.2: Surgery to repair is rhinoplasty. Rhinoscopy is diagnostic examination, not surgery.
wrongExplains.3: Removing polyps is polypectomy (surgical). Rhinoscopy is examination, may identify polyps but doesn't remove them.

scaffolds.narrative: Pollen grains are everywhere in Mr. Edgar's nasal passages. His immune system is overreacting. 'Allergic rhinitis,' Ms. Frizzle announces. 'Hay fever. The nose is ground zero for the allergic response.'
scaffolds.explain: ALLERGIC RHINITIS = allergic reaction in nasal mucosa. Allergens (pollen, dust mites, pet dander) → IgE response → histamine release → inflammation, rhinorrhea, congestion, sneezing. NOT infection - it's immune overreaction.

wrongExplains.0: Bacterial infection would be bacterial rhinosinusitis. Allergic rhinitis is IMMUNE-MEDIATED, not infectious.
wrongExplains.2: Fungal infection is rare in the nose (fungal sinusitis in immunocompromised). Hay fever is ALLERGIC.
wrongExplains.3: Structural issues (deviated septum) cause obstruction. Allergic rhinitis is inflammatory, not structural.

scaffolds.narrative: 'Rhinoplasty versus rhinitis,' Ms. Frizzle quizzes. 'One is medical, one is surgical. One treats disease, one reshapes structure. Learn your suffixes!'
scaffolds.q: How does rhinoplasty differ from rhinitis?
options: Rhinoplasty is nose surgery; rhinitis is nasal inflammation
Rhinoplasty is inflammation; rhinitis is surgery
scaffolds.explain: RHINOPLASTY = RHINO (nose) + PLASTY (surgical repair/reconstruction) = NOSE JOB (cosmetic or functional). RHINITIS = inflammation. Different suffixes, different meanings. '-plasty' = surgery, '-itis' = inflammation.

wrongExplains.0: Completely different! One is surgical, one is inflammatory. Different suffixes reveal different meanings.
wrongExplains.3: Neither is inherently infection. Rhinitis CAN be from infection, but rhinoplasty is always surgery.

scaffolds.narrative: The bus observes a rhinovirus particle latching onto a nasal epithelial cell. 'Rhinovirus,' Ms. Frizzle says. 'The most common cause of the common cold. Named for its target - the nose.'
scaffolds.q: Rhinovirus is so named because it:
options: Primarily infects the nose and upper respiratory tract
scaffolds.explain: RHINOVIRUS primarily infects the NOSE and upper respiratory tract. It's the leading cause of the common cold. Named for its primary replication site (nasal epithelium). There are >100 serotypes.

wrongExplains.0: The shape has nothing to do with rhinoceros. It's named for where it infects - the nose (rhino-).
wrongExplains.2: Not found in rhinoceros. Rhino- refers to the human NOSE, not the animal.
wrongExplains.3: Rhinovirus prefers UPPER respiratory tract (nose, pharynx), not lungs. It's a 'cold' virus, not pneumonia.

scaffolds.narrative: 'Rhinosinusitis,' Ms. Frizzle points out. 'Notice - TWO prefixes. Rhino- AND sinus-. When the nose and sinuses are both inflamed, we combine the terms.'
scaffolds.explain: RHINOSINUSITIS = inflammation of BOTH nose (rhino-) AND sinuses (sinus-). Often they're inflamed together because they're anatomically connected. Modern preferred term over 'sinusitis' alone, since the nose is usually involved.

wrongExplains.0: That would be just 'rhinitis'. Rhinosinusitis includes BOTH nose and sinuses.
wrongExplains.2: That would be 'sinusitis'. The 'rhino-' prefix adds the nose to the picture.
wrongExplains.3: Throat is pharyngo- or laryngo-. Rhinosinusitis is nose + sinuses, not throat.

scaffolds.narrative: The bus exits the nasal cavity, passing through the nasopharynx. 'The journey continues,' Ms. Frizzle says. 'But it all starts with rhino- - the nose. Final question!'
scaffolds.explain: 'RHINO-' = NOSE. From Greek rhis, rhinos. Used in: rhinitis (nasal inflammation), rhinorrhea (runny nose), rhinoscopy (nasal exam), rhinoplasty (nose surgery), rhinovirus (virus infecting nose). Master this prefix and dozens of terms make sense instantly.

wrongExplains.0: Throat uses 'pharyngo-' (pharynx) or 'laryngo-' (larynx). Rhino- is the nose.
wrongExplains.2: Lungs use 'pneumo-' or 'pulmo-'. Rhino- is the nose, the very first part of the airway.
wrongExplains.3: Sinuses use 'sinus-'. Rhino- is the nasal cavity itself, though nose and sinuses are connected.
